coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots with concentration-tunable multicolored photoluminescence for simultaneous detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yanbing
Chang, Qing
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: We presented multicolored emission carbon dots using for simultaneous detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions through assigning a different colored emission carbon dot to a different metal ion. Carbon dots were prepared by a facile hydrothermal treatment of the mixed solution of m-phenylenediamine, ethanol and ammonia. Their photoluminescence (PL) gradually evolved three strongest peaks with concentration reduction, and these strongest PL peaks exhibited different response patterns for Fe3+ and Cu2+ with high selectivity and sensitivity. Accordingly, the concentration-modulated multicolor carbon dots could be used to multimodal sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Multimodal sensing
Tunable photoluminescence
Chemical sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: PL spectra of (A) R-CDs, (B) G-CDs, and (C) B-CDs at different excitation wavelengths, and their photos (in the right) under the ambient light and 405nm laser beams, respectively.
FTIR spectra of R-, G-, and B-CDs (A) and their XPS full spectrum (B) C1s spectrum (C), N1s spectrum (D).
TEM (A) and HRTEM (B) images of R-CDs; TEM (C) and HRTEM (D) images of B-CDs.

(A) The strongest PL peak changes of B-, G-, and R-CDs in the presence of 16 kinds of metal ions (1mM) at excitation with 365, 460, and 500nm, respectively. (B) PL spectra of B-, G-, and R-CDs adding different concentrations of Fe3+ or Cu2+ ions (0, 10, 25, 50, 100, 200, 300, 500, 700, and 1000μM), respectively. (C) The linear relationships of the strongest PL peak changes of B-, G-, and R-CDs (F/F0) with the concentrations of Fe3+ or Cu2+ ions.
Schematic origins of concentration-dependent PL behaviors from CDs in the absence and presence of metal ions.
PL lifetime comparisons of R-, G- and B-CDs in ethanol solution in the absence and presence of Cu2+ or Fe3+ ions.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots with concentration-tunable multicolored photoluminescence for simultaneous detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We presented multicolored emission carbon dots using for simultaneous detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions through assigning a different colored emission carbon dot to a different metal ion. Carbon dots were prepared by a facile hydrothermal treatment of the mixed solution of m-phenylenediamine, ethanol and ammonia. Their photoluminescence (PL) gradually evolved three strongest peaks with concentration reduction, and these strongest PL peaks exhibited different response patterns for Fe3+ and Cu2+ with high selectivity and sensitivity. Accordingly, the concentration-modulated multicolor carbon dots could be used to multimodal sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much attention because of their many potential applications, particularly in chemical and biological sensors or tracers [1–6]. Typical metal ions including of Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ag+, and Pb2+ have been selectively recognized utilizing CD-based fluorescence probes [7–11]. In general, the fluorescence emission from CDs can be efficiently quenched by these metal ions [12–16]. Such quenching provoked by metal ions could be due to static quenching resulting from the formation of a non-fluorescent complex between the surface groups of CDs and metal ions [17–19]. For instance, the highly sensitive detection of Cu2+ ions was attributed to their coordination reaction with both the carboxylic acid and amino groups on the surfaces of CDs [20–22]. Although many efforts (e.g. modifying CDs with organic compounds and polymeric materials) have been made to improve the sensitivity of the assays, CD-based fluorescence probes are still lack the ability to detect simultaneously two or more kinds of metal ions, and hence not suitable for multimodal sensing so far.

Multicolored emission CDs could allow simultaneous detection of two or more metal ions through assigning a different colored emission CD to a different metal ion. Recently, a great progress has been made in tuning fluorescence emission of CDs. Hu et al. [23] obtained multicolored emission CDs, which exhibit fluorescence across to the whole visible spectrum excited by white light, through changing the contents of O and N. According to similar mechanism of tunable colored emission, Guo’s [24] and Ding’s [25] groups developed different methods towards multicolor emission CDs. Interestingly, Lin [26] group reported three colored CDs, i.e. red, green and blue emissions, using ethanol solution of p-phenylenediamine (pPD), o-phenylenediamine (oPD) and m-phenylenediamine (mPD), respectively. Three different phenylenediamine isomers could be involved in different carbonization process and hence resulted in the variations of size and N content in three colored CDs. These variations were believed to be responsible for different colored emissions of CDs.

In the current work, we presented on the fabrication of concentration-tunable multicolor CDs by only ethanol solution of mPD. Not only do the obtained CDs emit three different colors with their concentration changes, but also show the ability to detect Fe3+ and Cu2+ simultaneously with high selectivity and sensitivity.

Concentration-tunable multicolor CDs were prepared by the same process as Ref. [26] except for only using mPD as carbon source and adding ammonia. The photoluminescence (PL) properties of the obtained sample depended on the concentration of CDs as well as excitation wavelengths. Fig. 1 displays the PL spectra of the ethanol solution with 909, 61, and 6mgL−1 CDs through progressively longer excitation wavelengths from 340 to 540nm. The strongest PL peaks of these three samples are around 610, 520, and 450nm and the corresponding samples are named as R-, G-, and B-CDs, respectively. Particularly, these three strongest PL peaks are barely shifted when the concentrations of R-, G-, and B-CDs are varied in 250–1800, 50–200 and 4–20mgL−1 (Fig. S1 in Supporting information). The right of Fig. 1A–C shows the photos of R-, G- and B-CDs under the ambient light and 405nm laser beams, respectively. One can observe different emission colors. From above PL characterizations, it can be confirmed that PL emission from CDs is blue-shifted with their concentration reduction.

According to UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs (Fig. S2 in Supporting information), which display the increase of visible light absorption with the concentration, the observed concentration-dependent PL behaviors of CDs may result from fluorescence inner filter effect (IFE). Fluorescence IFE arises from photon absorbers and scatters in the sample solution and its degree is highly associated with the sample absorption intensities at the excitation and emission wavelengths [27,28]. Based on the as-acquired UV–vis and PL spectra of different concentrations of R-CDs (Fig. S3A and B in Supporting information), the IFE-corrected PL spectra were obtained by using the reported correction method [27,28] (Fig. S3C). The peak PL intensities of the as-acquired spectra reduced with the increase of CD concentration and showed a poor linear correlation to the CD concentration (Fig. S3D). After correction, theoretically, the PL intensities should be unchanged with CD concentrations if IFE is the only factor in determining PL behaviors of CDs [27]. However, the peak intensities of the IFE-corrected PL spectra were enhanced linearly by increasing CD concentrations (Fig. S3D), implying there should be other reasons for concentration-tuned PL of CDs besides IFE.

Since there are no differences in R-, G- and B-CDs except for containing different concentrations of CDs, their chemical compositions and structures are unlikely changed. To confirm this, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were performed. From Fig. 2 A, there are no fresh FTIR peaks with the concentration variations. The broad band from 3320 to 3510cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of NH and OH [29]. The band around 1662cm−1 is ascribed to the vibrational absorption band of CO and/or CN bonds [30]. The vibrational absorption bands of CN and CO bonds are present at around 1350 and 1080cm−1, respectively [31]. The chemical compositions of R-, G- and B-CDs are the same based on the XPS characterizations, in which C, N and O contents are 75.8, 16.9 and 7.3%, respectively (Table S1). As shown in Fig. 2B, C1s, N1s and O1s peaks are observed in the full survey, respectively. The C 1s spectrum is deconvoluted into four peaks, corresponding to the sp2 CC/CC bonds (284.4eV), CN bonds (285.5eV), CO bonds (286.3eV), CO/CN bonds (288.0eV) respectively. The N1s spectrum is also deconvoluted into three peaks of pyridinic type (398.6eV), amino type (399.0eV) and pyrrolic type (400.2eV) N atoms. According to previous reports, these heteroatom groups play an important role in tuning band gaps of an isolated carbon dot [23–25]. Inversely, concentration-modulated PL could be related with the interaction between CDs rather than heteroatom incorporations.

The morphologies of the CDs in different concentrations were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 3 A shows TEM image of R-CDs. Large pieces of the aggregations are dispersed in small nanoparticles. By high resolution TEM (HRTEM) observation (Fig. 3B), the large aggregation constitutes with several nanoparticles accompanying with distinct interface between them. In contrast, the TEM image of B-CDs indicates that the CDs are obviously mono-dispersed and exhibit a uniform size without predominant aggregation (Fig. 3C). Further HRTEM image in Fig. 3D reveals the same crystalline characteristic as shown in Fig. 3B. Hence the aggregation of CDs originates from their concentrations in ethanol solution.

Strikingly, three concentration-modulated CDs possess different features to sensing metal ions. As different kinds of metal ions of 1mM are added to sample solution, the strongest PL peaks of R- and B-CDs are obviously quenched in the presence of Cu2+ and Fe3+, respectively, while the strongest PL peaks of G-CDs increase with Fe3+ concentration (Fig. 4 A). By contrast, other metal ions such as Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Li+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Sr2+ and etc., barely influence the PL emissions from three concentration-modulated CDs. Upon changes of Fe3+ concentrations as shown in Figs. 4 B, S5 and S6, the intensity of the strongest peak emission from B-CDs displays continuous quenching, whereas that from G-CDs shows continuous enhancing. Unlike Fe3+ ions, the variations of Cu2+ concentrations only give rise to obviously continuous quenching of the strongest peak emissions from R-CDs. Additionally, the sensitivity of R-CDs on the temperature in the absence and presence of Cu2+ ions was also observed. As shown in Fig. S7, the PL intensity at 610nm reduce slightly with temperature increase in the absence of Cu2+ ions, but it remains almost unchanged in the range of 20–60°C when Cu2+ ions are present. This implies that the effects of temperature on the probe sensitivity can be negligible.

For Fe3+ ions, the quenching efficiency (F/F0) of B-CDs exhibits an excellent linear relationship ranging from 0 to 1000μM, while the enhancing PL efficiency (F/F0) of G-CDs gives an good linear relationship from 0 to 200μM. For Cu2+ ions, their concentration changes from 0 to 300μM can lead to linear quenching efficiency of R-CDs (Figs. 4 C and S5). In addition, their linear relationships follow Stern–Volmer equation, i.e. F0/F=1+Ksv[Fe3+ or Cu2+], (where F0 is the PL intensity without metal ion, F is the PL intensity in the presence of Fe3+/Cu2+, and Ksv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant). Three Stern–Volmer equations are F/F0 (B-CDs)=−0.0034−0.0017C(Fe3+) and the corresponding correlation coefficient R2 =0.9975, F/F0(G-CDs)=0.9937+0.0009C(Fe3+) and R2 =0.9778, F/F0(R-CDs)=−0.0088−0.0037C(Cu2+) and R2 =0.9821, respectively. According to theses equations, the detection limits for B-, G- and R-CDs are 0.19, 0.61, 0.12μM, respectively.

Based on all above results, a plausible mechanism was proposed for concentration-dependent PL behaviors from CDs in the absence and presence of metal ions. Initially, dehydrolysis process happened between ethanol and mPD molecules to generate nitrogen-rich CDs with abundant hydroxyl and carbanyl groups on the surface under solvothermal condition [23]. These primary CDs had blue fluorescence via the radiative recombination of the excited electrons from the n–π* transitions of CO [23,24]. As shown in Fig. 5 , some of them could further reacted with remaining mPD in the solution through carbanyl groups, forming mPD-grafted CDs. Surface-grafted mPD by CN bonds altered localized electronic states in CDs and then created new PL centers, yielding G-CDs. As the concentration increases, strong intermolecular forces caused the aggregation of mPD-grafted CDs. Consequently, the aggregation-induced electronic states gave rise to a new radiative recombination pathway in nanoaggregates [32], thus forming R-CDs.

The sensing mechanism for different metal ions could be attributed to energy level alignment between the different PL centers in CDs and the d state orbits of different metal ions. The combination of surface groups with metal ions through coordinating or chelating interactions bridged the electron transfer (ET) from PL centers to the d state orbits of metal ions [33,34]. Because the metal-centered d-d states of Fe3+ ions were likely situated below the n–π∗ states of CO in CDs, the PL from B-CDs was quenched by surface-chelating Fe3+ ions via photo-induced ET action [33]. For G-CDs, the Fe3+ ions were expected to prevent the ET from the benzene ring to lack electron body (CN) that favored nonradiative decay [35]. Thus, the emission of G-CDs was enhanced with the addition of Fe3+ ions. Similar to B-CDs, the d state levels of Cu2+ ions could be matched with the PL centers of R-CDs, and hence delocalized the photo-induced electrons in R-CDs. As a result, the PL of R-CDs was quenched by Cu2+ ions.

PL decay has been used for explaining sensing mechanism of CDs [36]. Here we also measured the PL lifetimes of the R-, G- and B-CDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+ or Fe3+ ions (Fig. S8). The PL lifetime data in Table 1 indicated that the average lifetime increased with the concentration decrease of CDs. According to the above discussion, the PL centers from R and G-CDs were different from those in B-CDs. Surface-state-induced traps were mainly PL mechanism for R- and G-CDs and thus ET process (which is accompanied by shortened PL lifetime) should be involved [24], making their PL lifetime shorten. After adding 1mM Fe3+ ions, the PL lifetime of B-CDs was significantly shortened, suggesting the presence of ET from B-CDs to Fe3+ ions [34,36]. By contrast, the PL lifetime of G- and R-CDs had less change after adding analytes since ET process had been involved in fluorescence emission. Therefore, PL decay also lent support to the proposed mechanism mentioned above.

Although more research is still needed to reveal why R-, G, and B-CDs show different response patterns for Fe3+ and Cu2+, this work offers a new strategy at least for the discrimination and simultaneous detection of multiple metal ions. Traditional methodologies generally require complicated fabrication or instruments to acquire sensing signals, thus restricting their applications in harsh and intricate environments. By contrast, multicolor fluorescence sensing platform fabricated by concentration-modulated CDs not only is reachable with low cost and possesses high accuracy and capability in diverse environments, but also can be used to directly analyze and trace reaction processes on the basis of the concentration change of CDs.

Concentration-dependent multicolor PL CDs were prepared using the mixed solution of mPD, ethanol and ammonia as a precursor via hydrothermal method. Diluting as-prepared CDs with ethanol, there presented three strongest PL peaks at around 615, 520, and 450nm, respectively. Such three PL centers exhibited different response patterns for Fe3+ and Cu2+, possessed detection limits of ppm as fluorescence probes. Therefore, concentration-modulated multicolor emission CDs could be used for multimodal sensing with high accuracy and capability in diverse environments.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. U1510125, 51272301, 51502270), Program for the Outstanding Innovative Teams of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi, Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, and North University of China Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.07.031.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Photoluminescence-tunable carbon nanodots: surface-state energy-gap tuning

Hydrothermal synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from sodium citrate and their use for the detection of mercury ions
A fluorescent nanosensor based on graphene quantum dots-aptamer probe and graphene oxide platform for detection of lead (II) ion
Economical: green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
Graphitic carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for highly efficient detection of Fe3+ ions
Preparation of highly photoluminescent sulfur-doped carbon dots for Fe(iii) detection
A fluorescent biosensor based on carbon dots-labeled oligodeoxyribonucleotide and graphene oxide for mercury (II) detection
Highly sensitive and selective detection of nanomolar ferric ions using dopamine functionalized graphene quantum dots
Highly photoluminescent nitrogen-rich carbon dots from melamine and citric acid for the selective detection of iron(iii) ion
Quick speciation of iron(ii) and iron(iii) in natural samples using a selective fluorescent carbon dot-based probe
Cyclam-functionalized carbon dots sensor for sensitive and selective detection of copper(II) ion and sulfide anion in aqueous media and its imaging in live cells
Facile preparation of fluorescent polydihydroxyphenylalanine nanoparticles for label-free detection of copper ions
Green synthesis of carbon dots from prawn shells for highly selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
Tunable photoluminescence across the entire visible spectrum from carbon dots excited by white light
Full-color light-emitting carbon dots with a surface-state-controlled luminescence mechanism
Tunable multicolor carbon dots prepared from well-defined polythiophene derivatives and their emission mechanism
Red green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging
Interaction between fluorescein isothiocyanate and carbon dots: inner filter effect and fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Using water Raman intensities to determine the effective excitation and emission path lengths of fluorophotometers for correcting fluorescence inner filter effect
Highly fluorescent carbon dots as selective and sensitive on-off-on probes for iron(III) ion and apoferritin detection and imaging in living cells
Controlling speciation of nitrogen in nitrogen-doped carbon dots by ferric ion catalysis for enhancing fluorescence
Synthesis separation, and characterization of small and highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots

Selective and sensitive fluorescent turn-off chemosensor for Fe3+
Carbon quantum dot-based nanoprobes for metal ion detection
Design: properties and application of a facile fluorescence switch for Cu(II)
Amine-rich carbon nanodots as a fluorescence probe for methamphetamine precursors

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yanbing Wang received his BS degree from Liaocheng University in 2015. Now he is pursuing his MS study at North University of China under supervision of Prof. Shengliang Hu. He current research focuses on developing multicolored carbon dots for sensing applications.

Qing Chang received her PhD degree from Northeastern University in 2011. She is working in College of Materials Science and Engineering of North University of China as an associate professor now. Her current interests are surface/interface design and synthesis of carbon dots for catalysis and sensing applications.

Shengliang Hu received his PhD degree from Tianjin University in 2008. He is working in College of Materials Science and Engineering of North University of China as a professor now. He was elected Science and Technology leader in 2014 and Sanjin Scholars of Shanxi Province in 2016. His research mainly focuses on the synthesis, modification, and application of carbon-based quantum dots.
